title: The Watchful Householder
subtitle: The hour of the thief, and the soul keeping vigil against death

stillness: The hour is hidden, that it may always be suspected.
While you cannot foresee it, prepare for it without ceasing.
gospel: that if the householder knew at what hour the thief would come,
and would not let his house be broken through.
for at an hour you know not the Son of Man will come.
What I say to you, I say to all: Watch.
behold: Hear how Chrysostom unfolds this likeness in two ways.
In the first: the thief is the devil; the house is your mind.
If he enters through the negligence of reason, the doorkeeper,
he strips that house of the garments of justice,
of the gold of wisdom, and the silver of innocence.
A man lets his own house be broken through
when, consenting to the devil's suggestions, he is stripped of virtues and wounded in his strength.
First the devil strikes by suggestion,
The first can scarcely be guarded against, the second more so, the third most of all.
In the other way: the thief is the day of death, for it comes secretly, like a thief;
the house is the body; the door is the mouth and the ears; the windows are the eyes.
Death is rightly called a thief:
for its unforeseen coming and hidden entry,
and for its stripping away of all things.
consider: The death of sinners is the worst of deaths.
Hear Chrysostom: sinful souls take their delight in the body as in their own dwelling.
When the angel of death comes with the sentence of God, he digs through the body,
and tears the soul violently away from it.
But the just souls, who do not delight in the body but are there as if in exile,
when the sentence comes, are not dug through unwilling, but called, rejoicing.
Hear Gregory: while the spirit sleeps from its own watch,
death comes unforeseen and bursts into the dwelling-place of our flesh,
and the one it finds sleeping, the master of the house, it slays;
for while he does not foresee the ruin to come, death drags him, unknowing, to torment.
He would resist the thief, if he kept watch,
meeting the Judge with penance, that he might not perish unrepentant.
The Lord willed the last hour to be unknown to us, that it might always be suspected.
If such diligence is spent guarding a small thing,
how much more must be spent guarding the soul.
In whatever state your last day finds you, in that the last day of the world will hold you fast.
Augustine: let no one ask when the last day shall be.
Where we are found, there we shall be judged.
Every Christian in death shall see Christ, and then know whether he is of the elect or the damned.
The evil man sees Christ to his shame and confusion,
blushing that, by his own fault, he was not redeemed by the blood of Christ.
Of such it is said: They shall look on me whom they have pierced.
But the good man sees Him for rejoicing.
O how happy is he who foresees his last things,
who guards himself from sins, who does not neglect himself,
who prepares for death at every hour,
so that whenever death comes, it finds him ready, and receives him rejoicing,
as one who awaits the longed-for coming of his deliverer,
saying with the Apostle: I desire to be dissolved, and to be with Christ!
prayer: grant me unceasingly to ponder the day of my passing and the day of judgment,
and to foresee my last things,
that I may watch carefully for things spiritual and eternal,
guarding myself from sins, and pressing on in good works;
that I may not neglect myself,
but at every hour prepare and dispose myself for my death.
Grant, O Lord, that I may provide each day for my own ending,
and that my whole life may be such in its living
that I may deserve to be free in the end,
and to receive You, coming in death or in judgment, rejoicing,
and to rejoice with You without end. Amen.

latinJewel.latin: Horam vero ultimam Dominus noster idcirco nobis voluit esse incognitam, ut possit semper esse suspecta, ut dum illam praevidere minime possumus, ad illam sine intermissione praeparemur.
latinJewel.english: Our Lord willed the last hour to be unknown to us for this reason: that it might always be suspected, so that, while we are least able to foresee it, we might prepare for it without ceasing.
